item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The force behind it sort of likes humans more than it likes the Grokodus, in the same way that you like a chicken more than you like that asshole who cut you off in traffic. But it doesn't think humans are creatures deserving of much moral consideration, much like you're not particularly upset about the aforementioned jerk driver having participated in a much graver wrong to the chicken that was in her lunch sandwich.

This is slightly complicated by the force behind this being a fan of Christianity, but it (a) isn't a very good Christian, and (b) still doesn't really care about any other individual Christians.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Can a Grokodus law be deliberately made?

What would happen if a law mandating Grokodus communicate were to be passed?

I wonder if the SCP Foundation has enough power to push a law like this through a minor country's parliament.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this one, it's odd and charming. I'd be happy to read a huge supplemental log of other Grokodus laws.

Mobile Task Force Pi-4

No nickname though? Poor Pi-4. :(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's also the possibility that it has to eat to live, and letting it die would be worse somehow- say, releasing it from the bindings entirely or something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It could just be that (keeping in mind how long ago Old English was used) the people consumed weren't considered particularly important as long as the population remained steady, and as long as it didn't eat "one of Ours", presumably the royal family and/or parliament.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The way the excerpted laws are written makes it seems as if the force behind the laws like humans more than it like the Grokodus. However, the Sustenance law lets it murder 10 humans a day. So, does the force behind the laws not realize the consequences of its laws? Does it have a much different understanding of cause-and-effect than us mere mortals? Or is it only pretending to like humans, when it reality it doesn't give a crap?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Quite a bit late here, but I just want to say that this was the scp that convinced me to get an account here in the first place. It's one of my favorites, definitely. Definitely a +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know this is a bit random, but did anyone else notice that this is the fifth SCP out of five keters right in a row?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like how weird and interesting this is, but I think the weakest part of it is that the laws just magically appear. A more interesting origin, even just vague allusions to one, would be preferable. Maybe play on how arcane legal systems can get, and have the dog and the laws keep showing up because the laws just exist, undocumented, in the law of loads of different countries because they all based their legal systems on whichever ancient state originally punished the dog in the first place.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Downvote for some iffy and confusing prose. Let me give a few examples:

"SCP-2079-A is the set of laws drawn on during incidences of SCP-2079. Though these laws are in a 10th century dialect of Old English and do not conform to modern legal standards, nothing unusual about this is observed by members of the affected organization. Persons involved in the passage of an SCP-2079-A instance show thorough understanding of that instance, but they demonstrate no additional comprehension of similar Old English writings."

Suggested re-write:

Each law or set of laws that is proposed and adopted with respect to SCP-2079 is referred to as an instance of SCP-2079-A. The text of each instance of SCP-2079-A is in a 10th century dialect of Old English, regardless of the normal working language of the legislative body in question. Moreover, instances of SCP-2079-A are typically inconsistent with the the drafting styles and usages of the legislative body that enacts it. Nonetheless, members of that legislative body demonstrate thorough understanding of the meaning and legislative intent of the instance of SCP-2079-A that they debate and adopt even though those members demonstrate no additional comprehension of similar Old English writings.

And so on. A couple of general notes:
"enacted at an increasing rate, which varies greatly…" This is confusing: if the rate is increasing, then it goes without saying that the rate varies. But does adding this information improve your piece?

"approximately fifty laws". Does this mean fifty separate legislative enactments? Or does it count if the legislative body enacts a single law with fifty separate substantive paragraphs? I'm a lawyer, so I got kind of hung up on the "how do you count how many laws there are" issue, which is probably an undesirable distraction from the general gist of your piece, which I take to be "after a few legislative enactments, the actual Grokodus shows up".

Why do the laws have to be in Old English, anyway? It's kind of disruptive to suspension of disbelief- because in-universe, you'd expect the addendum to contain the actual text of the law in question (not a modern translation). You could get around this by having the laws be adopted in modern English, but have the addendum specify that the laws have been adopted in lots of different times and places, including ones where you wouldn't expect the laws to be adopted in Modern English (like, say, 10th century Korea, or modern Tunisia, or whatever).

I would suggest re-organizing your addendum to make it clearer, in each case, which legislative body is adopting the law in question.

For some reason, I'm reminded of SCP-1377.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's no real progression to what happens in the article, true. It's a lot less narrative than, say, SCP-2762, but I wasn't really aiming in that direction. I think the reader's understanding of the Grokodus and its situation should develop over the course of the article, especially in light of the caustic tone of the laws themselves. It's not narrative, but I'd say it's a story, if admittedly a slight one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is the second Eldritch being that I want to hug today. Like, I want to know what Grok did to deserve special legislation, and tell him that it'll be ok. Of course, he might flay me and eat my eyes, so I'd be careful to read the bylaws.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea a lot, which keeps me from downvoting, but I can't help but feel like you have a setup with no payoff here. It's just a description of the effect without any twist or anything, which is a little boring. Also, there are some minor tone problems. Like, for example, "containing powerful jaws" seems a bit too informal to me.

Neutral vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Arguably, this is my least abstract Keter yet. The other two can't cause anyone any physical harm at all.

A supplement with more laws could well work. I might even be able to allow people to submit their own, if I could clearly enough specify the tone and qualities I'm going for.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Man, Keters are getting more abstract by the day.

Anyway, while I don't quite get the fourth law, I love the text of them and wish there was a big document somewhere with more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This, of course, will lead to counter ads…
"My opponent is clearly a Grokodus sympathizer. I, however, voted for the passage of no fewer than 5 laws to control our eldritch abomination issue…"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The juxtaposition is interesting. :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea of a being which is willed into existence by anomalous legislation is just plain intriguing, and though the article feels a little thin overall, I'm willing to upvote for originality.

Now I'm just going to sit back and imagine Congressmen giving their campaign speeches about how the Senate spent hundreds of hours last year debating Grokodus mating habits instead of reforming Social Security.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just feel like I've seen it all before. The laws manifesting, bringing about its existence by basically believing in it (in a roundavout way.) It's very well-written as usual, but it just doesn't do it for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I did like it, well written and explained in good detail. Some elaboration on the wine production would be cool.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I initially thought this was kind of silly and found many of the gothic tropes narm-y, it had me smiling by the end. +1

Also, the version I read in RSS had some grammatical issues. I'll check back when I'm at a computer to see if I can point anything out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I particularly liked the note mentioning the Boy Scouts.

Yes, I'm a bad person like that. Upvoted for everything else.
